project_overview.project_development_objective: Support the Lebanese Republic restore the basic functions of domestic revenue mobilization and accountable allocation and use of public resources
project_overview.total_financing: US$34 million (US$28.5 million IBRD loan + US$5.5 million Lebanon Financing Facility grant)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Primary: Ministry of Finance, Court of Accounts, Central Inspection, procurement institutions and their staff. Secondary: Lebanese citizens through improved public service delivery and fiscal management

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Lebanon is experiencing severe economic and financial crisis since October 2019, exacerbated by COVID-19 pandemic and August 2020 Port of Beirut explosion. Real GDP contracted 34% between 2018-2022. Currency lost 98% of value. Triple-digit inflation. Public finances collapsed with revenues declining from 13.1% GDP (2020) to 6.1% GDP (2022). Foreign currency reserves depleting. Political deadlock and governance challenges present. Project designed to operate despite ongoing political stalemate.

key_components.description: Support for revenue functions including Large Taxpayer Office strengthening, customs restoration, and revenue IT systems maintenance/upgrades. Includes operational support, training, capacity building, and solar panel installation (~US$1 million).
key_components.financing: US$14.1 million (US$10.6 million loan + US$3.5 million grant)

key_components.description: Support for budget preparation, fiscal and financial reporting, payment processing, and cross-cutting ICT functionalities. Includes technical assistance, training, equipment, and solar panels (~US$1 million).
key_components.financing: US$12.4 million (US$10.4 million loan + US$2 million grant)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Revitalizing Oversight and Accountability Institutions and Procurement Capacity Building
key_components.description: Strengthen Court of Accounts and Central Inspection capacity, enhance procurement training and professionalization. Includes operational support, technical assistance, IT equipment, and solar panels (~US$0.8 million).
Support for Project Coordination Unit establishment and staffing, monitoring and evaluation, communications, and independent verification of performance-based conditions.
Provision for immediate response to eligible crisis or emergency events affecting public or financial sector, if needed during implementation.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Finance (MoF) with Project Coordination Unit (PCU) established within MoF. PCU will include Project coordinator, financial officer, procurement officer, environmental and social specialist, and monitoring and evaluation specialist. Implementation also involves Court of Accounts, Central Inspection, Institute of Finance, and Public Procurement Authority.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by Minister of Finance, established within 3 months of effectiveness, comprising heads of relevant agencies. Periodic development partner coordination forums. 3RF Working Group on Anticorruption, PFM, Civil Service and Public Administration Reform serves as consultative forum. Advisory committee with civil society and academia representatives.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF (September 2023). High-risk prior review threshold due to market instability, price fluctuations, exchange rate volatility. Advance procurement allowed. Retroactive financing available for eligible expenditures from November 1, 2023 to signing date (ceiling US$2 million). STEP system used for tracking. Procurement Plan to be updated regularly. Anti-Corruption Guidelines apply. Enhanced forced labor declarations required for certain procurements.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Results framework with PDO and intermediate indicators. Performance-Based Conditions (PBCs) verified by independent verification agency (IVA) based on verification protocols. PCU responsible for overall M&E coordination. Semi-annual implementation reports. Online dashboard for stakeholders. External independent auditor conducts annual financial audits. M&E Officer in PCU coordinates data collection from implementing entities. Quarterly Interim Financial Reports (IFRs) submitted to Bank within 45 days of quarter end.

identified_risks.risk_description: High governance and political risks inherent to Lebanon due to confessional governance system, political uncertainty, and ongoing political deadlock limiting government's ability to take expedient policy actions. Potential to negatively impact restoration of public services.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong ownership from Prime Minister and Minister of Finance offices and agency heads. Project interventions focused on MoF and specific independent agencies designed to be implemented despite political stalemate. Continuous stakeholder engagement. Technical design with operational support strengthened by PBCs.

identified_risks.risk_description: Severe macroeconomic situation with deteriorating currency, triple-digit inflation, depleting foreign reserves, and weak government capacity to pay adequate salaries. Government unable to ensure staff availability for Project implementation due to economic constraints.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project will pay allowances for staff to attend work and achieve key deliverables. Enhanced DRM and public expenditure management will help lay foundations for macroeconomic stabilization by creating fiscal space. Medium-term revenue and customs measures and strategic budget allocations for fiscal sustainability.

identified_risks.risk_description: While government committed to PFM reforms in Financial Recovery Plan 2022, comprehensive master plan for implementation not in place. Risk of uncoordinated support from international partners.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Coordination among international community partners, governmental institutions, and stakeholders. PCU to facilitate periodic development partner coordination meetings. 3RF Working Group serves as forum for coordination. Project design focuses on urgent and critical activities.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk from urgency and complexity of reforms, volume of work, sustainability challenges, and ability to exit from paying staff allowances. Reintroducing neglected practices (budgeting, reporting, audits) at time of high absenteeism and staff turnover is risky.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Rewarding achievement of results through PBCs. Operational funding to facilitate staff attendance. Essential capacity building. Preventing further deterioration of critical PFM functions. Allowance scheme established as temporary and subject to regular reviews. Medium-term impact of PFM restoration activities expected to improve fiscal space for sustainable wage reforms.

identified_risks.risk_description: High risk from number of stakeholders and vested interests. Reforms (strengthening LTO, risk-based customs audits, transparency, accountability) involve changes affecting stakeholder interests, creating win-lose situations preventing universal acceptance. Lack of development partner coordination mechanism and overall masterplan may result in duplication.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Continuous engagement with all stakeholders. Political economy analysis informed Project design. Development partner coordination forums. Despite mitigation measures, residual risk remains high.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Public services crippled due to staffing issues (attrition, absenteeism). Implementation capacity weak. Staff unable to afford commuting and basic necessities due to wage erosion from currency devaluation.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Short-term capacity provided through temporary financial support to critical staff. Temporary measures to implement critical actions for government's crisis response. Medium- to long-term sustainability measures including medium-term revenue and customs measures and strategic budget allocations for fiscal sustainability and growth.

identified_risks.risk_description: Combined fiduciary risk rated high. Financial management risk substantial due to overall country situation, elevated corruption perception, limited public servant availability, currency deterioration, delays in transfers/payments, limited capacities of oversight institutions. Procurement risk high due to market instability, price fluctuations, exchange rate volatility, narrow competition, lengthy transactions, dysfunctional banking sector.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Preparation of Project Operations Manual with financial management chapter. Hiring of fiduciary staff (financial officer, procurement officer). Appointment of independent verification agency. Recruitment of external auditors. Ring-fenced system for transaction recording. Direct payment method available. External auditor verification of staff allowance eligibility. Spot checks by external auditors. Robust internal control processes. High-risk prior review threshold for procurement.

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate risk. Project does not involve infrastructure or major civil works. Overall associated with positive social impacts.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Standards ESS1, ESS2, ESS3, ESS4, ESS10 apply. Stakeholder Engagement Plan and Environmental and Social Commitment Plan prepared and disclosed. Labor Management Procedures, Occupational Health and Safety Plan, and Waste Management Plan to be prepared within 3 months of effectiveness. E&S specialist appointed part-time within 2 months of effectiveness. Solar panel investments assessed for environmental and social impacts.

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level grievance mechanism established. MoF building on existing grievance uptake channels to meet ESS10 requirements. Phone line maintained to receive complaints incorporated in broader grievance redress mechanism. Grievance procedures publicized among citizens and stakeholders. Semi-annual review of complaints received and responses to identify vulnerabilities. World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism also available to project-affected communities.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Lebanon ranks 132 of 146 countries in Global Gender Gap Index 2023. Limited visibility of gender issues in national budget. Project will establish processes during budget preparation linking National Commission for Lebanese Women (NCLW) strategic gender objectives to national budget allocations. Gender Impact Assessment to be carried out with Mashreq Gender Facility (MGF) technical experts. Gender-responsive budgeting guidelines to be prepared. Gender annex on budget allocations to be included in budget report submitted to Parliament. Training on gender-sensitive public procurement implementation decrees planned. Female participation in citizen engagement activities tracked and encouraged. Procurement training includes topics on women-owned enterprises and gender-sensitive implementation.
vulnerable_groups: Public sector staff affected by wage erosion and currency devaluation
Pensioners dependent on timely pension payments
Poor and vulnerable populations disproportionately affected by inflation
Women (underrepresented in economic participation and political empowerment)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project designed to address fragility drivers in Lebanon. Focuses on restoring trust and renewing social contract between State and citizens through improved governance, transparency, and accountability. Strengthens public financial management and procurement systems to support core public services. Supports domestic revenue mobilization to create fiscal space for growth-enhancing investments and debt reduction. Improves institutional quality and vertical trust through strengthening effectiveness, reliability, efficiency, transparency and accountability of public administration. Project activities (strengthening LTO, risk-based customs audits, greater transparency, accountability) involve changes affecting stakeholder interests but are essential for crisis response and recovery. Project designed to operate despite ongoing political stalemate and can be implemented at technical level to limit implementation bottlenecks.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Lebanon experiencing most devastating multipronged crisis in modern history since October 2019. Real GDP contracted 34% between 2018-2022. Lebanese pound lost 98% of value. Triple-digit inflation (171.2% in 2022, projected 231% in 2023). Foreign currency reserves depleting (US$8.4 billion as of September 2023, down US$22.5 billion since October 2019). Public finances collapsed - revenues fell from 20% GDP (2018) to 6.1% GDP (2022), one of lowest globally. Public expenditures fell from 16.4% GDP (2020) to 9% GDP (2022). Banking sector insolvent. Government unable to borrow. Fiscal deficit 2.9% GDP (2022). Years of weak fiscal discipline, lack of transparency, structural issues in financial governance. Long-standing institutional capacity weaknesses. High informality (shadow economy ~35% GDP). Tax evasion endemic. Unintegrated tax organization. Government budget not explicitly policy-oriented. 13-year period (2005-2017) without passed budget. Staff attrition and absenteeism crippling public institutions. Currency devaluation eroded public sector salaries making them unaffordable for basic necessities. Significant skills gap in public sector. High absenteeism rates. Customs and tax revenues fell sharply. VAT and customs revenues experienced biggest declines. Volume and value of foreign trade halved 2019-2020. Severe staff shortages prevent revenue-critical activities. Lack of funding for daily running costs of government offices. No national budget for 2023. Treasury advances at all-time high. Fiscal information and audit reports unavailable. Backlog of year-end financial statements not being cleared. In-year fiscal information unavailable since 2022. Public accountability eroded by delays in operationalizing Public Procurement Authority and Procurement Complaint Authority. Systematic training of public procurement workforce not deployed.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project document notes country context does not account for unfolding regional events since October 7, 2023. Project designed to operate despite ongoing political deadlock and instability. Implementation focused at technical level to limit bottlenecks from political uncertainty. Project activities focused on MoF and specific independent agencies designed to be implemented even if political stalemate continues. Strong ownership from current Prime Minister and Minister of Finance offices and agency heads provides some security for implementation. Project aims to restore core government functions essential for effective crisis response and stabilization.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: no - Project does not involve land acquisition, involuntary resettlement, or displacement. ESS 5 (Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement) marked as not currently relevant. However, Lebanon context includes significant refugee and displaced populations from Syrian crisis and Palestinian refugees, though project does not directly address displacement issues. Project's improved public financial management and fiscal space creation could indirectly support services for vulnerable populations including refugees.
